questions.question: What is the 'sustainability paradox' in service?

options.text: More service always leads to better outcomes
People who commit to massive service often quit entirely, while those who commit to small, consistent service sustain for years
Service is never sustainable
Only professionals can sustain service long-term

questions.explanation: The paradox: grand commitments rely on motivation (fleeting), while small habits create systems (durable). 'I'll volunteer 20 hours/week!' often leads to burnout and quitting. 'I'll volunteer 2 hours every Saturday' builds a habit that lasts years. Motivation is unreliable; habits are robust. This lesson's goal: build service you'll still be doing a year from now, not just this month. Consistency matters more than intensity.

questions.question: What are 'implementation intentions' and why do they double success rates?

options.text: Vague goals like 'I'll help more'
Specific when/where plans: 'When/Where X happens, I will do Y'—removes decision-making friction
Intentions don't matter; only action does
Complex multi-step plans

questions.explanation: Implementation intentions follow format: 'When/Where X happens, I will do Y.' Example: 'Every Saturday at 9am, I will tutor at the library' (vs. vague 'I'll volunteer more'). Research shows specific when/where plans double success rates because they: remove decision fatigue (no 'Should I go today?'), create automatic triggers (Saturday 9am → tutoring), and make tracking easier. The specific trigger + specific action removes friction.

questions.question: According to habit research, what should you do if you miss your service habit once?

options.text: Give up; the habit is broken
Get back on track immediately—missing once is normal; missing twice starts a broken habit
Feel intense shame and guilt
Wait until next month to restart

questions.explanation: Habit research is clear: miss once, get back on track immediately. Missing a single occurrence doesn't break a habit—it's normal. Missing twice in a row is the start of a broken habit pattern. Avoid shame (counterproductive). Instead: acknowledge the miss, identify what got in the way, adjust if needed, and do the habit as soon as possible. The faster you resume, the more durable the habit becomes. Perfection isn't required; consistency is.

questions.question: What are the six elements that make service sustainable?

options.text: Money, fame, recognition, awards, social media, publicity
Right-sized commitment, clear boundaries, intrinsic motivation, meaningful feedback, social connection, recovery time
Working alone, no limits, guilt-driven, constant availability, no breaks, intensity
Only doing service when motivated

questions.explanation: Six sustainability elements: (1) Right-sized commitment (matches actual capacity), (2) Clear boundaries (defined scope, start/end times), (3) Intrinsic motivation (values-driven, not guilt), (4) Meaningful feedback (seeing impact), (5) Social connection (serving with others), (6) Recovery time (rest between service). These create durable service. Their opposites create burnout: overcommitment, no boundaries, guilt-driven, isolation, constant service without recovery.

questions.question: When should you scale back or adjust your service commitment?

options.text: Never; quitting shows weakness
When service causes stress vs. fulfillment, other life areas suffer, you're exhausted not energized, you feel resentful, or you're neglecting your own needs
Only when someone forces you to stop
Service should always be stressful and depleting

questions.explanation: Signs to scale back: service causes stress rather than fulfillment, other areas (family, work, health) suffer, you're consistently exhausted not energized, you feel resentful/obligated not willing, you're meeting others' needs while neglecting your own. Adjusting isn't quitting—reducing hours, changing roles, taking breaks, or stopping one commitment for another is okay. Capacity changes with life circumstances. Service should enhance life, not consume it. Check in regularly and adjust without guilt.
